title: Land under cereal production (hectares)
definition: Land under cereal production refers to harvested area, although some countries report only sown or cultivated area. Cereals include wheat, rice, maize, barley, oats, rye, millet, sorghum, buckwheat, and mixed grains. Production data on cereals relate to crops harvested for dry grain only. Cereal crops harvested for hay or harvested green for food, feed, or silage and those used for grazing are excluded.

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata
source.sourceOrganization: FAO electronic files and web site, Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), uri: https://www.fao.org/faostat/en/#data/QCL, note: Item code F1717, publisher: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO)

editorial.shortHook: A raw grain-area map for expert review.
editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include India, China, United States, while the lightest include Djibouti, Bahamas, The, Qatar; that contrast is the fastest way to read the land under cereal production pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Land under cereal production can show major grain belts, but raw-area maps need careful editorial handling.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Arable land area can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Agriculture nitrous oxide emissions can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Cereal production can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Expert difficulty: the map can be mistaken for adjacent indicators unless players probe a high, low, and middle country.
editorial.dataCaveat: Raw land-area totals favor large countries and may be less fair than shares.
regionalSignals: Land-use maps are strongest when you compare deserts, forests, and farming belts.
Large countries can hide very different land-use shares.

reviewNotes: Batch 3 promotion: strong grain-belt pattern and broad coverage, useful for expert Daily variety.
Raw hectares favor large countries, so keep this as expert-facing and reveal the raw-area caveat.
